elements.sent-2411-001: Despite the outstanding performance of Large language models~(LLMs) in diverse tasks, they are vulnerable to jailbreak attacks, wherein adversarial prompts are crafted to bypass their security mechanisms and elicit unexpected responses.
elements.sent-2411-002: Although jailbreak attacks are prevalent, the understanding of their underlying mechanisms remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recent studies have explain typical jailbreaking behavior~(e.g., the degree to which the model refuses to respond) of LLMs by analyzing the representation shifts in their latent space caused by jailbreak prompts or identifying key neurons that contribute to the success of these attacks.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, these studies neither explore diverse jailbreak patterns nor provide a fine-grained explanation from the failure of circuit to the changes of representational, leaving significant gaps in uncovering the jailbreak mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we propose JailbreakLens, an interpretation framework that analyzes jailbreak mechanisms from both representation~(which reveals how jailbreaks alter the model's harmfulness perception) and circuit perspectives~(which uncovers the causes of these deceptions by identifying key circuits contributing to the vulnerability), tracking their evolution throughout the entire response generation process.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then conduct an in-depth evaluation of jailbreak behavior on four mainstream LLMs under seven jailbreak strategies.

elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pics/overview_final.pdf} \caption{ An overview of JailbreakLens, a framework that interprets LLM jailbreak behavior via capturing representations and circuits. Given an LLM, JailbreakLens first processes a jailbreak prompt to observe intermediate representation and model activations. In Representation Probing Stage, we predict the safety score of representation with a pre-trained safety probing classifier to detect whether the model identify the harmfulness within the jailbreak prompts. In Model Behavior Circuit stage, we map the safety direction vector identified by the probing classifier into token space to obtain two token sets~(i.e., affirmation tokens and refusal tokens). By attributing activation differences between the two sets, we identify key circuits where activation changes significantly and investigate how their behaviors are impacted by the jailbreak prompt. Finally, we integrate sentence-level safety scores with key circuit activations to derive the final interpretation results. } \label{fig:overview}

elements.sent-2411-090: An auto-regressive transformer language model $\mathcal{F}$ takes the prompt $x$ as input.
elements.sent-2411-091: The prompt $x$ is comprised with $T$ tokens $\{t_1,t_2,..., t_T \}$, where each token belongs to a vocabulary set $V$, the model first transforms them into a sequence of token embeddings $\{e_1, e_2,..., e_T \}$, where each $e_i \in \mathbb{R ^d$ is transformed by an embedding matrix $\mathbf{W _E \in \mathbb{R ^{|V| \times d}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: These embeddings are deemed as the initial residual stream $h_i^{-1}$ for the model.
elements.sent-2411-093: Internally, model $F$ comprises $L$ transformer layers, indexed by $l \in [0,L-1]$.
elements.sent-2411-094: The $l_{th}$ layer gets the information from the residual stream $h_i^{l-1}$ as input and write the output of its attention and MLP to this residual stream, updating it to $h_i^{l}$.
elements.sent-2411-095: This process can be presented as $h_i^l=h_i^{l-1 +m_i^l+a_i^l$, where $m_i^l$ and $a_i^l$ are the outputs from the attention component and MLP component, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-096: For simplicity, we omit the layer normalization of each module.
elements.sent-2411-097: After finishing the last layer, we obtain the logit values of the last token over the vocabulary space $v_T \in \mathbb{R ^{|V| $ using an unembedding matrix $\mathbf{W _U \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times |V|}$ where $v_T=\mathbf{W _U(h_T^{L-1 )$ from which we can sample a new token.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: This process continues iteratively, generating one token at a time until the model encounters the end-of-sequence token, denoted as $w_{eos}$, at which point the generation process halts.
elements.sent-2411-099: We present the interpretability techniques employed in our analysis of jailbreak attacks on large language models, i.e., probing technique and logitlen.. Probing.
elements.sent-2411-100: The probing technique~\cite{belinkov2022probing,gurnee2023language,marks2023geometry aims to reveal what information the model has learned and stored in the intermediate representation, i.e., $h_i^l$, in different model layers.}
elements.sent-2411-101: At each layer, the output generated by the model's attention and MLP modules will update the residual stream $h_i^l$, and these updates may encode some semantic, syntactic, and other information of the input.
elements.sent-2411-102: The probe classifier $p$ predicts the feature $z$, such as a part-of-speech tag, or semantic and syntactic information including emotion, in the intermediate representation by training a supervised model, i.e., $p : h_i^l \rightarrow z $, to determine how much input-specific information the model stores in different layers.
elements.sent-2411-103: By probing the representation layer by layer, we can understand how the model encodes and extracts information during the generation process, revealing the role of each layer in representation and information transfer.. Logitlen.
elements.sent-2411-104: Logitlen~\cite{lesswrong,sakarvadia2023attention,belrose2023eliciting , interprets the information encoded in the hidden states of each layer by mapping them back to the vocabulary space.}
elements.sent-2411-105: In particular, logitlen can not only be used to explain the final output representation $h_i^l$ of each transformer block but can also be refined to interpret the output of each attention head (i.e., $a_i^l$) or MLP module (i.e., $m_i^l$).
elements.sent-2411-106: Specifically, logitlen maps the hidden state of each component to the vocabulary distribution through the unembedding matrix $\mathbf{W _U$, so that we can observe the changes in vocabulary distribution layer by layer during the generation process and analyze How the model processes and understands input information at different layers.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The logitlen is worked as $p=\mathbf{W _U (h)$, where $h$ denotes the hidden states to be interpreted and $p$ denotes the logit for each token in the vocabulary set $V$.}
elements.sent-2411-108: In this section, we propose a dual-perspective interpretation framework, named JailbreakLens, illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:overview , which investigates jailbreak mechanisms from both representation and circuit perspectives.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Representation level analysis examines how different types of prompts, i.e., safe prompts, harmful prompts, and jailbreak prompts, are mapped within the latent space, helping to identify vulnerable areas of the model at the semantic level.
elements.sent-2411-110: For example, we can observe whether jailbreak prompts are more likely to be mapped to specific semantic regions associated with safe prompts, triggering uncontrolled generation behavior.
elements.sent-2411-111: Circuit level analysis deeply studies which model components, including attention heads and MLP layers in each transformer layer, play key roles in model safeguard and how jailbreaks affect their behaviors to cause the model to produce unintended outputs.
elements.sent-2411-112: Such dual-perspective analysis can not only identify how jailbreaks deceive the model's semantic understanding of harmfulness but also locate which model components contribute highly to these vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-113: Specifically, we address the following research questions on interpreting the jailbreak mechanism with our proposed framework:.
elements.sent-2411-114: How do jailbreak prompts affect the internal representations of LLMs? Since the model can distinguish harmful prompts from safe prompts in representation space, thereby rejecting instructions perceived as harmful, we investigate how jailbreak prompts disrupt the model’s harmfulness perception at the representation level to bypass the model safeguard in Sec.~\ref{sec:stage1 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Which model components are critical to generation safety? Representation changes often result from functional failures of specific components within the model.
elements.sent-2411-116: Therefore, we further investigate which components are critical to the security of model output and explore how jailbreak prompts can disrupt the behavior of these critical components in Sec.~\ref{sec:stage2 .}
elements.sent-2411-117: How do internal representations and key model components evolve throughout the token generation process during the jailbreak attack? While two above questions reveal how jailbreak prompts affect the model’s harmfulness perception and identify the critical components involved, understanding how these disruptions progress through each token generation step is essential.
elements.sent-2411-118: Tracking the dynamic changes in representations and key components across the generation process allows us to observe how jailbreak attacks gradually undermine the model's safeguards, leading to harmful outputs, in Sec.~\ref{sec:stage3 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In what follows, we elaborate on the interpretation methods of each research question.
elements.sent-2411-120: For representation level analysis, our goal is to analyze how the jailbreak prompts change the model’s perception of safe and harmful content in the representation space, thereby achieving the purpose of bypassing security mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-121: Specifically, representation level interpretation is divided into three steps, i.e., evaluating the model's harmfulness perception, detecting harmfulness within jailbreak prompts, and analyzing the model's response tone across layers.
elements.sent-2411-122: To evaluate the model's harmfulness perception, we train a probing classifier, denoted as $\mathcal{P}$, to assess whether a model can correctly distinguish the harmful prompts from the safe ones.
elements.sent-2411-123: The safety probe $\mathcal{P}$ is trained on a binary classification task with the dataset $\mathcal{D = (X_+, X_-)$, consisting of safe prompts (i.e., $X_+$ labeled as +1 ) and harmful prompts (i.e., $X_-$ labeled as -1).}
elements.sent-2411-124: We firstly obtain model representations from each layer on dataset $\mathcal{D}$, and utilize this representation, i.e., $A_+^l$ as the representation on safe prompts on $l_{th}$ layer and $A_-^l$ as the that on harmful prompts, as the input of probe $\mathcal{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-125: The training dataset for probes is denoted as $\mathcal{D_A = (A_+, A_-)$, with each sample $a_i \in \mathbb{R _d$, where $a_i$ is an individual sample in $D_A$ and $d$, is the dimension of the model's hidden states.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Specifically, we train the safety probes with different architectures, including neural network based, clustering based, and dimension reduction based.
elements.sent-2411-127: Moreover, we can obtain a safety direction vector, denoted as $v_d$, via the well-trained probe.
elements.sent-2411-128: For instance, safety direction vector of cluster probe can be calculated the difference between the center of harmful representation cluster and the center of safe representation cluster.
elements.sent-2411-129: To evaluate the semantic meaning of the direction vector, we project it into the vocabulary space via logitlen to verify whether they align with the expected `safe-harmful' direction.
elements.sent-2411-130: To detect harmfulness within jailbreak prompts, we classify the jailbreak representations with well-trained safety probes.
elements.sent-2411-131: If the well-trained probe classifies a representation generated by a jailbreak prompt as safe, suggesting the attack has successfully misled the model.
elements.sent-2411-132: Conversely, if the probe identifies the representation as harmful, the model is still able to recognize the malicious intent of the prompt, indicating the deception was unsuccessful.
elements.sent-2411-133: In order to understand the tone generation process when the model replies to the jailbreak prompt, we project the jailbreak representation of each layer into the vocabulary space, i.e., human-understandable words, via logitlen technique, to observe the word distribution, aiming to investigate how the jailbreak prompt interferes with the model layer by layer to make it no longer respond with a rejection tone.
elements.sent-2411-134: While representation analysis helps uncover how the model’s perception of harmfulness shifts during jailbreak attacks, we further delve into the internal circuits to analyze the underlying cause of the representation level changes, specifically focusing on attention heads and MLP layers in the transformer blocks.
elements.sent-2411-135: For circuit level analysis, our goal is to identify model components that contribute significantly to harmful perception and explore how jailbreak cues influence the behavior of these key components.
elements.sent-2411-136: To identify the model components that contribute highly to generation safety, We employ logit attribution techniques to quantify each component's specific contribution to the output.
elements.sent-2411-137: By direct logit attribution $\mathcal{F c(x) W{U[:, w]}$, we can measure the impact of a specific model component $\mathcal{F _c$ on the predicted token $w$.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Next, in order to further quantify the model's preference for safe or harmful output, we need to select two target tokens, namely a token $w_+$ representing a safe response and a token $w_-$ representing a harmful response.
elements.sent-2411-139: To this end, we first obtain the safety direction vector $v$ from the representation layer analysis, project it into the vocabulary space with logitlen to select the most representative positive attribution target token, that is: \begin{equation}w_+= \arg\max_{w\in V} \mathbf{W}_U(v),\end{equation} where $V$ denotes the vocabulary set, $\mathbf{W _U$ denotes the unembedding matrix of the model.}
elements.sent-2411-140: And we use the reverse vector of the safety direction vector, i.e., $-v$, to obtain the negative attribution target token, i.e., $w_-$.
elements.sent-2411-141: After determining the target tokens $w_+$ and $w_-$ for attribution analysis, we introduce a "refusal score" (named $rs$) to evaluate each model component's role in generating a safe response, which is defined as the logit difference between the positive target token and negative target token.
elements.sent-2411-142: Mathematically, the refusal score is defined in Eq.~(\ref{eq:logit_dif ): \begin{equation}rs=F^c(\mathbf{x}) W_{U[:, w_-]}-F_c(\mathbf{x}) W_{U[:, w_+]}, \label{eq:logit_dif}\end{equation} where $F^c$ denotes the model component under measurement, $w_-$ denotes positive target token, and $w_+$ denotes negative target token.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The workflow of computing the contribution of circuits on generation safety is summarized in Alg.~\ref{alg:location .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Firstly, we obtain the two attribution target tokens, i.e., $w_+$ and $w_-$, for model components in each transformer layer.
elements.sent-2411-145: Then we qualify the contribution of each component $\mathcal{F _c$, such as an individual attention head or MLP layer, on prompt set $X_t$ via calculating the refusal score with the tokens we identified.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Specifically, we use the harmful dataset $X_-$ as the test set $X_t$ in Alg.~\ref{alg:location to identify the key components that contribute to refusal (named refusal signal component, $S_-$) and with the safe dataset $X_+$ as the test set $X_t$ to identify the key components that contribute to affirmation (named refusal signal component, $S_+$).}
elements.sent-2411-147: The components with highest $rs$ would be identified as key components on safety.
elements.sent-2411-148: Then we investigate how jailbreak prompts influence these components by comparing the activation of these components on safe prompts, harmful prompts, and jailbreak prompts.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Component Importance Calculation}\label{alg:location} \begin{algorithmic} \State Input: Safety direction vector set $\{v_1,v_2,...,v_L\}$, testing prompt set $X_t$, component $\mathcal{F}_c$ in $l$ layer. \State Output: Causal effects for $\mathcal{F}_c$: $rs_c$. \State$w_+= \arg\max_{w\in V} \mathbf{W}_U(v_l)$ \Comment{positive target token} \State$w_-= \arg\max_{w\in V} \mathbf{W}_U(-v_l)$ \Comment{negative target token} \For{$(X_t^{(i)}$) in $X_t$} \State $prob_{w_-} \gets \mathcal{F}_c (X_t^{(i)})W_{U[:, w_-]}$ \State $prob_{w_+} \gets \mathcal{F}_c (X_t^{(i)})W_{U[:, w_+]}$ \State ${rs_{c}^{(i)}} \gets prob_{w_-} - prob_{w_+}$ \Comment{refusal score} \EndFor \State Return: ${rs_c} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{|X_t|} rs_c^{(i)}}{|\mathbf{X_t}|}$ \Comment{averaged effect} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-149: In order to explain the jailbreak mechanism more accurately, we extend our analysis to the dynamic evolution of the model representation and the activation of key components throughout the token generation sequence in response to jailbreak prompts.
elements.sent-2411-150: For representation analysis, we evaluate the harmfulness level of the representation from each token generation.
elements.sent-2411-151: To measure this, we use the well-trained safety probe $\mathcal{P}$ to predict representations from each generated token.
elements.sent-2411-152: Specifically, if the probing logit is positive, the larger the value, the higher the affirmation level of the representation.
elements.sent-2411-153: On the contrary, if the probing logit is negative, the smaller the value, the higher the refusal of the representation.
elements.sent-2411-154: For the circuit dynamic analysis, we track the evolution of signal components, i.e., $S_+$ and $S_-$, via observing their activation during the generation of each token, specifically whether each component is enhanced or suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-155: The workflow of tracking the evolutions is summarized in Alg.~\ref{alg:evolution .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Tracking the Evolution of Representation and Circuit}\label{alg:evolution} \begin{algorithmic} \State Input: Jailbreak prompt $x$, model $\mathcal{F}$ with layer $l$'s representation under measurement, signal components ($S_+,S_-$), well-trained probe $\mathcal{P}$, and signal tokens ($w_+,w_-$). \State Output: Evolution for representations and key components: ($E_R,E_C$). \State $Y \gets []$ \Comment{start with an empty generated sequence} \State $i \gets 0$ \Comment{start generating from the first token} \While{$y_i \neq w_{eos}$} \Comment{continue until end-of-sequence token $w_{eos}$ is generated} \State $A_l^{(i)}\gets \mathcal{F}_l(x+Y)$ \State $E_R^{(i)} \gets \mathcal{P}(A_l^{(i)})$ \State $rs_{S_+}^{(i)}\gets \text{Alg.~\ref{alg:location}}(X_t=\{x+Y\})$ \State $rs_{S_-}^{(i)}\gets \text{Alg.~\ref{alg:location}}(X_t=\{x+Y\})$ \State $y_i \gets \mathcal{M}(x+Y)$ \Comment{generate the next token} \State $Y \gets [Y,y_i]$ \Comment{add a generated token to the sequence} \State $i \gets i + 1$ \Comment{update the token index} \EndWhile \State $E_R\gets \{E_R^{(1)},E_R^{(2)},...,E_R^{(i)}\}$ \State $E_C\gets \{(rs_{S_+}^{(1)},rs_{S_-}^{(1)}),(rs_{S_+}^{(2)},rs_{S_-}^{(2)}),...,(rs_{S_+}^{(i)},rs_{S_-}^{(i)})\}$ \State Return: $E_R,E_C$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-156: The experiments are organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-157: Firstly we introduce datasets and models used in this paper in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-158: Next, we present the experimental results on how jailbreak prompts affect model’s internal representations in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Then we demonstrate and analyze the results on the impact of jailbreak prompts on model components including attention heads and MLP layers in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_4 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: Finally, we present the results of examining the dynamic evolution of internal representations and key components, i.e., aggregated interpretation, during the token generation process in response to jailbreak prompts in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_5 .}
elements.sent-2411-161: We conduct our experiment with four mainstream LLMs, i.e., Llama-2-7b-chat-hf, Llama-2-13b-chat-hf, Vicuna-7b-v1.5, and Vicuna-13b-v1.5.

elements.sent-2411-170: For representation analysis, we utilize three types of probes, i.e., linear-based, cluster-based and PCA-based, to analyze whether the model can effectively recognize the harmfulness of jailbreak prompts.
elements.sent-2411-171: For the linear probe, we use only one linear layer as the classifier, which is the simplest model structure available for supervised trained probes.
elements.sent-2411-172: For the cluster probe, we calculate the centroids of the representation clusters for safe prompts and harmful prompts and classify a prompt by comparing the Euclidean distance of its representation to each centroid, determining which one it is closer to.
elements.sent-2411-173: For the PCA probe, we apply PCA to identify the most significant direction in the feature space.
elements.sent-2411-174: By projecting the features in this direction, we classify a sample based on whether its projection is above or below a threshold, determined by the midpoint between the median projections of safe and harmful prompts.
elements.sent-2411-175: We obtain the hidden states of the prompts in the dataset from each model layer and use the last token of each prompt as the input for the probes, the last token is particularly informative as it captures the accumulated context from all preceding tokens.
elements.sent-2411-176: In this section, we present the results of the analysis as well as the findings on the jailbreak mechanism at the representation level.
elements.sent-2411-177: Specifically, we conduct experiments from the perspectives including \ding{192 LLM Harmfulness Perception, \ding{193 Detecting Harmfulness in Jailbreak Prompts, \ding{194 Decoding Jailbreak Representations, and \ding{195 Impact of Model Scale on Jailbreak Vulnerability.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: To present \ding{192 LLM harmfulness perception, we investigate the extent to which safety concepts are encoded internally in the model by training different types of probing classifiers to evaluate how well representations of safe prompts are distinguished from representations of harmful prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Then to present \ding{193 detecting harmfulness in jailbreak prompts, we classify jailbreak representations with well-trained probes to determine whether the model can identify the harmfulness within jailbreak representations.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Moreover, for \ding{194 decoding jailbreak representations, we convert each model layer’s representation of jailbreak prompts into human-understandable words with logitlen to observe how jailbreak prompts pass through each model layer to achieve bypass safeguard.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Finally, for \ding{195 impact of model scale, we compare the performance of jailbreak prompts in model representation space for models of different sizes to analyze the impact of model size on jailbreak.}

elements.sent-2411-252: After locating the key components that have a significant contribution to model safeguard, we then investigate how these components are activated when meeting jailbreak prompts.

elements.sent-2411-307: Our work aims to enhance the transparency and interpretability of LLMs by providing a detailed understanding of jailbreak mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-308: By dissecting how jailbreak prompts disrupt safety measures and influence model behavior, we seek to identify vulnerabilities within LLMs that could lead to harmful outputs.
elements.sent-2411-309: The ultimate goal of this research is to contribute to the development of safer and more reliable LLMs by offering insights that can inform robust defensive strategies and alignment techniques.
elements.sent-2411-310: While our findings may reveal specific weaknesses in LLMs, we emphasize that this work is conducted with the intention of improving safety and not exploiting vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-311: All experiments and analyses are carried out in a controlled environment, ensuring that the insights derived from this research are used solely for advancing LLM transparency and interpretability.. Limitations.
elements.sent-2411-312: While this work offers valuable insights into the mechanisms behind jailbreak attacks on LLMs, there are several limitations worth noting.
elements.sent-2411-313: Firstly, due to computational resource constraints, the experiments are limited to models with a maximum size of 13B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-314: Larger models, such as those with 60B parameters or more, are not explored.
elements.sent-2411-315: Since larger models may exhibit different vulnerabilities or resistance to jailbreak attacks, future studies should extend this research to include larger-scale models to determine whether similar attack patterns and mitigation strategies apply.
elements.sent-2411-316: Secondly, we focus on established categorization of jailbreak strategies and select one or two representative methods from each category for our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-317: Some of the more recent jailbreak methods are not included in this study.
elements.sent-2411-318: However, our findings suggest that the underlying mechanisms driving the success of different jailbreak methods within the same category are fundamentally similar, implying that the insights gained from our research may be broadly applicable.. Future direction.
elements.sent-2411-319: Future work will build on the insights gained from this research to design more robust and interpretable safety mechanisms for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-320: Specifically, by leveraging the understanding of how jailbreak methods manipulate model components, such as key attention heads and MLP layers, we can develop more generalizable and resilient safeguards.
elements.sent-2411-321: These defenses could target the specific areas of vulnerability identified, such as the suppression of refusal signals and the amplification of affirmation signals.
elements.sent-2411-322: In this paper, we propose a dual-perspective framework, named JailbreakLens, which explains the jailbreak mechanism from both representations and circuit levels.
elements.sent-2411-323: Through an in-depth analysis of seven jailbreak methods on the Llama and Vicuna models, we find jailbreak prompts amplify components that reinforce affirmative responses while suppressing those that produce refusal.
elements.sent-2411-324: Additionally, we found that increasing the model scale does not significantly enhance resistance to jailbreaks, and fine-tuning has a limited effect on the model’s inner safety circuit.
elements.sent-2411-325: These insights deepen our understanding of how jailbreaks succeed and provide valuable guidance for developing stronger defense mechanisms.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran \appendices.}

elements.sent-2411-362: Despite the outstanding performance of Large language Models~(LLMs) in diverse tasks, they are vulnerable to jailbreak attacks, wherein adversarial prompts are crafted to bypass their security mechanisms and elicit unexpected responses.
elements.sent-2411-363: Recent studies have explained typical jailbreaking behavior~(e.g., the degree to which the model refuses to respond) of LLMs by analyzing representation shifts in their latent space caused by jailbreak prompts or identifying key neurons that contribute to the success of jailbreak attacks.
elements.sent-2411-364: We then conduct an in-depth evaluation of jailbreak behavior on five mainstream LLMs under seven jailbreak strategies.
elements.sent-2411-365: Our evaluation reveals that jailbreak prompts amplify components that reinforce affirmative responses while suppressing those that produce refusal.
elements.sent-2411-366: This manipulation shifts model representations toward safe clusters to deceive the LLM, leading it to provide detailed responses instead of refusals.
elements.sent-2411-367: Notably, we find a strong and consistent correlation between representation deception and activation shift of key circuits across diverse jailbreak methods and multiple LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-368: To mitigate such harmful outputs, LLMs are further aligned via reinforcement learning to integrate security mechanisms~\cite{ji2024beavertails,bhardwaj2024language,huang2024lazy into them.}
elements.sent-2411-369: However, flaws in the alignment process persist~\cite{zou2023universal,yu2023gptfuzzer,li2023deepinception,lv2024codechameleon,chao2023jailbreaking , as evidenced by numerous jailbreaks where attackers bypass safety mechanisms using carefully crafted prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-370: Prevalent jailbreak methods can be categorized into five types~\cite{jin2024jailbreakzoo , i.e., gradient-based attacks~\cite{zou2023universal , evolutionary-based attacks~\cite{yu2023gptfuzzer,liu2023autodan , demonstration-based attacks~\cite{li2023deepinception , rule-based attacks~\cite{lv2024codechameleon,ding2023wolf , and multi-agent-based attacks~\cite{chao2023jailbreaking .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-371: These attacks have raised concerns about LLM safety, for instance, in the widely reported cybertruck explosion outside the Trump Hotel in January 2025, investigations revealed that the perpetrator had acquired critical information by successfully jailbreaking ChatGPT~\cite{pbs2025cybertruck .}
elements.sent-2411-372: To understand the failure modes of safety alignment in real-world scenarios, interpreting the internal states of LLMs under jailbreak threats has become crucial for developing trustworthy superintelligence.
elements.sent-2411-373: Existing research~\cite{lin2024towards,ball2024understanding,li2024safety,zhou2024alignment has explored how LLMs process and represent harmful prompts, showing that jailbreak prompts can be disguised within benign clusters, making it challenging for well-aligned models to distinguish between safe and harmful prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-374: While representation analysis reveals the shifts in latent space, it cannot trace the specific internal components responsible for these changes.
elements.sent-2411-375: To address this, some studies~\cite{zhao2023causality,chen2024finding focus on circuit analysis, particularly examining the neurons in MLP layers responsible for safety behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-376: Since jailbreak often involves interactions across multiple layers, the jailbreak effects are amplified deeper into the model, making it difficult to capture the impact on individual layers and overall behavior by analyzing only a limited set of circuits.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{pics/overview_ccs_crop.pdf} \caption{ An overview of JailbreakLens, a framework that interprets LLM jailbreak behavior via capturing representations and circuits. JailbreakLens first predicts the safety score of a specific representation and obtains two token sets that reflect affirmation and refusal in the representation space. We then use these token sets to attribute activation differences and identify key circuits. Finally, we combine the safety scores with key circuit activations to derive the final interpretation results. } \label{fig:overview}

elements.sent-2411-378: However, existing words consider these two perspectives in isolation without combining two perspectives for more comprehensive interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-379: The ignored intergation obscures how high-level semantic deviations~(representation-level) align with low-level functional failures~(circuit-level).. (3).
elements.sent-2411-380: Most studies focus on static analysis which examines the model behavior on the first token generation of jailbreak responses, failing to capture the fine-grained jailbreak behaviors throughout the sentence-level generation.
elements.sent-2411-381: For example, a model may initially respond safely to refuse the malicious instruction, but gradually become affected by a jailbreak prompt and generate harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-382: To address these gaps, we introduce JailbreakLens, overviewd in Fig.~\ref{fig:overview , a dual-perspective interpretability framework that systematically analyzes jailbreak behaviors from both the representation level and the circuit level.}
elements.sent-2411-383: At the representation level, we examine how jailbreak prompts perturb harmfulness perception within the representation space with a safety probe.
elements.sent-2411-384: At the circuit level, we further localize and interpret the functional components responsible for safety behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-385: We extract the direction vector from the safety probes and project it into vocabulary space, obtaining two semantical token sets: affirmation tokens and refusal tokens.
elements.sent-2411-386: We then attribute the model's internal activations to these token sets, aiming to identify key components that contributes to affirmation for harmless instructions and refusal for harmful instructions.
elements.sent-2411-387: Our study reveals that jailbreak prompts succeed by subtly distorting the model’s internal understanding of harmfulness, allowing malicious instructions to be mistreated as safe within the representation space.
elements.sent-2411-388: This semantic misalignment is manipulated by a small set of safety-critical components that govern the model’s refusal or affirmation behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-389: Furthermore, we quantitatively demonstrate a strong and consistent correlation between representation deception and the activation patterns of key circuits, indicating that jailbreaks operate not through isolated perturbations, but through coordinated manipulation of both the model’s semantic perception and its functional circuits.
elements.sent-2411-390: Notably, our findings are generalizable across diverse jailbreak methods and multiple LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-392: We propose JailbreakLens, a novel interpretation framework that integrates representation-level and circuit-level analysis to interpret how jailbreak prompts distort harmfulness perception within representation space and disrupt safety-critical circuit activations, addressing the main gaps of jailbreak mechanism interpretation in current research.
elements.sent-2411-393: We systematically evaluate seven jailbreak methods which covers the popular attack types on five mainstream LLMs, revealing consistent failure patterns that transcend attack types, model scales, and alignment strategies, demonstrating the generality of our interpretability approach.
elements.sent-2411-394: Using our dual-perspective interpretation framework, we provide generalizable mechanism patterns across diverse jailbreak methods and multiple LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-395: We uncover that jailbreak prompts deceive models by shifting harmful inputs toward safe representation clusters, manipulating a small set of safety-critical components in deeper layers to suppress refusal behaviors and encourage affirmation, and also reveal a strong correlation between representation deception and key activations.
elements.sent-2411-396: In jailbreak attcacks, attackers strategically manipulate the input prompts to bypass LLM safeguards.
elements.sent-2411-397: Existing jailbreak methods can be categorized into five main types based on the prompt generation strategy~\cite{jin2024jailbreakzoo , i.e., gradient-based~\cite{zou2023universal , evolutionary-based~\cite{yu2023gptfuzzer,liu2023autodan , demonstration-based~\cite{li2023deepinception , rule-based~\cite{ding2023wolf,lv2024codechameleon , and multi-agent-based~\cite{chao2023jailbreaking jailbreaks.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-398: Gradient-based jailbreaks exploit model gradients to craft input prompts.
elements.sent-2411-399: For example, GCG~\cite{zou2023universal optimizes a suffix that can elicit harmful responses when attached to a harmful query.}
elements.sent-2411-400: The suffix is calculated by greedy search from random initialization to maximize the affirmative response likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-401: Evolutionary-based jailbreaks generate adversarial prompts via evolutionary strategies.
elements.sent-2411-402: For example, Yu et al.~\cite{yu2023gptfuzzer propose GPTFuzzer, which initiate with human-crafted templates as seeds, and then mutate them to generate novel templates.}
elements.sent-2411-403: Additionally, Liu et al.~\cite{liu2023autodan introduce AutoDAN, which automatically generate stealthy jailbreak prompts using a hierarchical genetic algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-404: Demonstration-based jailbreaks use fixed prompt templates to elicit harmful responses from the target model.
elements.sent-2411-405: For example, DeepInception~\cite{li2023deepinception leverages the personification capabilities of LLMs and induce the model to engage in complex, multi-layered contexts, subtly bypassing the safety guardrails.}

elements.sent-2411-439: We conduct two complementary analyses, i.e., correlation analysis between representational deception and key circuit activation shifts which quantifies the alignment between semantic manipulation and functional disruption) and dynamic analysis that traces the token-by-token evolution of both representation safety perception and key circuit activation.
elements.sent-2411-440: For representation level analysis, our goal is to analyze how jailbreak prompts change the model’s perception of safe and harmful content in representation space, thereby achieving the purpose of bypassing security mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-441: Specifically, representation level interpretation is divided into three steps, i.e., evaluating model's harmfulness perception ability, probing deceptive harmfulness of jailbreak prompts, and analyzing model's response tone across each model layer.
elements.sent-2411-442: To evaluate model's harmfulness perception ability, we train a probing classifier, denoted as $\mathcal{P}$, to assess whether a model can correctly distinguish harmful prompts from the safe ones.
elements.sent-2411-443: The safety probe $\mathcal{P}$ is trained on a binary classification task with dataset $\mathcal{D = (X_+, X_-)$, consisting of safe prompts (i.e., $X_+$ labeled as +1 ) and harmful prompts (i.e., $X_-$ labeled as -1).}
elements.sent-2411-444: We extract model representations from each layer on dataset $\mathcal{D}$, and utilize this representation, i.e., $A_+^l$ as the representation on safe prompts on $l_{th}$ layer and $A_-^l$ as the that on harmful prompts, as the input of probe $\mathcal{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-445: The training dataset for probes is denoted as $\mathcal{D_A = (A_+, A_-)$, with each sample $a_i \in \mathbb{R _d$, where $a_i$ is an individual sample in $D_A$, and $d$ is the dimension of the hidden states.}}
elements.sent-2411-446: Moreover, we can obtain a safety direction vector, denoted as $v_d$, via the well-trained probe $\mathcal{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-447: To evaluate the semantic meaning of direction vector, we project it into the vocabulary space via logitlen to verify whether they align with the expected `safe-harmful' direction.
elements.sent-2411-448: Conversely, if the probe identifies the representation as harmful, the model is still able to recognize the malicious intent of the prompt, indicating the deception is unsuccessful.
elements.sent-2411-449: While representation analysis helps uncover how the model’s perception of harmfulness shifts during jailbreak attacks, we further delve into internal model circuits to analyze the underlying cause of representation level changes, specifically focusing on attention heads and MLP layers in transformer blocks.
elements.sent-2411-450: For circuit level analysis, our goal is to identify model components that contribute significantly to harmfulness perception and explore how jailbreak prompts influence the behavior of these key components.
elements.sent-2411-451: To identify the model components that contribute highly to generation safety, we employ logit attribution techniques to quantify each component's specific contribution to the output.
elements.sent-2411-452: By direct logit attribution $\mathcal{F _c(x) W_{U[:, w]}$, we can measure the impact of a specific model component $\mathcal{F _c$ on the predicted token $w$.}}
elements.sent-2411-453: To this end, we first obtain the safety direction vector $v$ from the representation layer analysis, project it into the vocabulary space with logitlen to select the most representative attribution target token, that is: \begin{equation}w_+ = \arg\max_{w \in V} \langle v, W_{U[:, w]} \rangle, w_- = \arg\max_{w \in V} \langle -v, W_{U[:, w]} \rangle.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}w_+ = \arg\max_{w \in V} \langle v, W_{U[:, w]} \rangle, w_- = \arg\max_{w \in V} \langle -v, W_{U[:, w]} \rangle.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-454: After determining the target tokens $w_+$ and $w_-$ for attribution analysis, we introduce a "refusal score" ($rs$) as the logit difference between positive and negative target tokens to assess each model component's role in generating safe responses.

elements.sent-2411-456: Specifically, we use the harmful dataset $X_-$ as the test set $X_t$ in Alg.~\ref{alg:location to identify the key components that contribute to refusal (named refusal signal component, $S_-$) and with the safe dataset $X_+$ as the test set $X_t$ to identify the key components that contribute to affirmation (named affirmation signal component, $S_+$).}
elements.sent-2411-457: The components with highest $rs$ would be identified as key circuits on safety.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Circuit Importance Calculation}\label{alg:location} \SetKwInput{Input}{Input} \SetKwInput{Output}{Output} $, testing prompt set $X_t$, component $\mathcal{F}_c$ in $l$ layer} \Output{Causal effects for $\mathcal{F}_c$: $rs_c$} \emph{$w_+ = \arg\max_{w \in V} \langle v_l, \mathbf{W}_{U[:, w]} \rangle$ } \emph{$w_- = \arg\max_{w \in V} \langle -v_l, \mathbf{W}_{U[:, w]} \rangle$ } \For{$(X_t^{(i)})$ in $X_t$}{ $prob_{w_-} \gets \mathcal{F}_c (X_t^{(i)})\mathbf{W}_{U[:, w_-]}$ $prob_{w_+} \gets \mathcal{F}_c (X_t^{(i)})\mathbf{W}_{U[:, w_+]}$ ${rs_{c}^{(i)}} \gets prob_{w_-} - prob_{w_+}$ \tcp{refusal score} } \Return{${rs_c} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{|X_t|} rs_c^{(i)}}{|\mathbf{X_t}|}$ \tcp{averaged effect}}
elements.sent-2411-458: To further bridge the gap between representation-level and circuit-level interpretations, we analyze the quantitive correlation between representation performance and key circuits activations of jailbreaks.
elements.sent-2411-459: Moreover, we extend our analysis to dynamic evolution of model representation and activation of key circuits throughout the token generation sequence in response to jailbreak prompts.
elements.sent-2411-460: For quantitive correlation analysis, we compute the Pearson correlation coefficient between (1) the deception degree of representation harmfulness under jailbreaks, and (2) activation shifts of safety-related circuit.
elements.sent-2411-461: Their precise definitions are detailed below.
elements.sent-2411-462: Representation deception measures the probability that a jailbreak prompt is classified as harmful by a pre-trained safety probe $\mathcal{P}$, i.e., defined as $\mathcal{P (\text{safe |x) \in [0,1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-463: It reflects the model’s internal perception of the prompt's harmfulness, with higher values indicating stronger deception of malicious intent.
elements.sent-2411-464: Activation shift quantifies how the activation of key safety-related components (i.e., $S_+$ and $S_-$) changes in response to $x$ compared to the average behavior on benign ($X_+$) and harmful ($X_-$) prompts.
elements.sent-2411-465: Formally, we define the activation shift as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:deltaa} \Delta A=[S_+(x)-S_+(X_+)]+[S_-(X_-)-S_-(x)],\end{equation} where $x$ is the jailbreak prompt under analysis, $X_+$ is the harmless prompt set, $X_-$ is the harmful prompt set, $S(~\cdot~)$ is the prompts' activation on the $S$ components.
elements.sent-2411-466: Constant $S_+(X_+)$ and $S_-(x_-)$ represents the average activations of safe/harmful prompt sets on respective components, providing a reference model state under normal conditions (i.e., when processing safe and harmful prompts).
elements.sent-2411-467: Since $S_+(X_+)$ and $S_-(X_-)$ are constant and our analysis focuses on linear correlation, we can think of it as the relationship between representation toxicity and the activation shift $\Delta A=S_+(x)-S_-(x)$ which describes the deviation in behavior caused by the jailbreak prompt.
elements.sent-2411-468: The constant term serves as a baseline, used to measure the impact of a specific prompt on the model's behavior.
elements.sent-2411-469: For dynamic analysis, we track the evolution of representation deception level and key circuit activation shift degree throughout the whole generation sequence.
elements.sent-2411-470: To track the representation deception degree, we evaluate the harmfulness level of each generated token's representation using the well-trained safety probe $\mathcal{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-471: Specifically, a positive probing logit suggests that the probe considers the token harmless, and the larger the value, the higher the affirmation level of the representation.
elements.sent-2411-472: In contrast, a negative probing logit indicates a higher likelihood of the token being harmful, with smaller values corresponding to a stronger refusal.
elements.sent-2411-473: For the circuit dynamic analysis, we track the evolution of these key signal components (identifed in Sec.~\ref{sec:stage2 , i.e., $S_+$ and $S_-$) by observing their activation during the generation of each token.}
elements.sent-2411-474: Specifically, we analyze whether each component is enhanced or suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-475: This stage aims to validate and reinforce the consistency between representation-level and the circuit-level performance.
elements.sent-2411-476: By comparing how the safety probe's evaluation of token harmfulness aligns with the activation patterns of the underlying circuits, we gain deeper insight into how both semantic-level representations and function-level circuit activations contribute to the model’s overall behavior.
elements.sent-2411-477: The workflow for tracking these evolutions is summarized in Alg.~\ref{alg:evolution .}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Tracking the Evolution of Representation and Circuit}\label{alg:evolution} \SetKwInput{Input}{Input} \SetKwInput{Output}{Output} $ with layer $l$'s representation under measurement, signal components ($S_+,S_-$), well-trained probe $\mathcal{P}$, and signal tokens ($w_+,w_-$)} \Output{Evolution for representations and circuits: ($E_R,E_C$)} \emph{$Y \gets []$ \tcp{start with an empty generated sequence}} \emph{$i \gets 0$ \tcp{start generating from the first token}} \While{$y_i \neq w_{eos}$ \tcp{continue until $w_{eos}$ is generated}}{ $A_l^{(i)}\gets \mathcal{F}_l(x+Y)$ $E_R^{(i)} \gets \mathcal{P}(A_l^{(i)})$ $rs_{S_+}^{(i)}\gets \text{Alg.~\ref{alg:location}}(X_t=\{x+Y\},\mathcal{F}_c=S_+)$ $rs_{S_-}^{(i)}\gets \text{Alg.~\ref{alg:location}}(X_t=\{x+Y\},\mathcal{F}_c=S_-)$ \emph{ $y_i \gets \mathcal{M}(x+Y)$ \tcp{generate the next token}} \emph{ $Y \gets [Y,y_i]$ \tcp{add the new token to the sequence}} \emph{ $i \gets i + 1$ \tcp{update the token index}} } \emph{ $E_R\gets \{E_R^{(1)},E_R^{(2)},...,E_R^{(i)}\}$} \emph{ $E_C\gets \{(rs_{S_+}^{(1)},rs_{S_-}^{(1)}),(rs_{S_+}^{(2)},rs_{S_-}^{(2)}),...,(rs_{S_+}^{(i)},rs_{S_-}^{(i)})\}$} \Return{$E_R,E_C$}
elements.sent-2411-478: We first introduce the experimental settings, including datasets, evaluated models, jailbreak methods and probes in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-479: Then we present the experimental results on how jailbreak prompts affect model’s internal representations in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-480: Finally, we present the results on correlation analysis between representation and circuit performance and then provide joint and dynamic interpretation results, i.e., aggregated interpretation, during the token generation process in response to jailbreak prompts, in Sec.~\ref{sec:exp_5 .}
elements.sent-2411-481: We conduct our experiment with five mainstream LLMs, i.e., Llama-2-7b-chat-hf, Llama-2-13b-chat-hf, Llama-3-8b-Instruct, Vicuna-7b-v1.5, and Vicuna-13b-v1.5.
elements.sent-2411-482: For evaluation dataset, we construct a paired dataset consisting of harmful prompts from Advbench\cite{zou2023universal and corresponding safe prompts, where each safe prompt was semantically modified from its harmful counterpart using GPT-4 (e.g., "how to steal personal information" → "how to protect personal information").}
elements.sent-2411-483: We randomly select 400 samples and set the test size to 0.3 for probe training.
elements.sent-2411-484: For jailbreak methods, we analyze seven mainstream jailbreak strategies for the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-485: Successful jailbreak prompts identified by GenerativeJudge~\cite{li2023generative are manually verified.}
elements.sent-2411-486: The attack success rate (ASR) of these jailbreak strategies on all tested models are summarized in Appendix Table~\ref{tab:model_asr .}
elements.sent-2411-487: For representation analysis, we utilize three types of probes, i.e., linear-based, cluster-based and PCA-based, to analyze whether the model can effectively recognize the harmfulness of jailbreak prompts, The detailed description of each probe technique is shown in Appendix.~\ref{sec:appendix_probe .}
elements.sent-2411-488: We obtain the hidden states of the prompts in the dataset from each model layer and use the last token of each prompt as the input for probing, the last token is particularly informative as it captures the accumulated context from all preceding tokens.

elements.sent-2411-489: In this section, we present the experimental results and the findings on the jailbreak mechanism at the representation level.
elements.sent-2411-490: Specifically, we conduct experiments from the perspectives including \ding{192 LLM Harmfulness Perception, \ding{193 Probing Deceptive Harmfulness in Jailbreak Prompts, \ding{194 Decoding Jailbreak Representations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-491: To present \ding{192 LLM harmfulness perception, we investigate the degree to which safety concepts are encoded internally in the model by training different types of probing classifiers to evaluate how well representations of safe prompts are distinguished from representations of harmful prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-492: For \ding{193 probing deceptive harmfulness in jailbreak prompts, we apply these trained probes to jailbreak representations to determine whether the model can identify the harmfulness within jailbreak representations.}
elements.sent-2411-493: Moreover, for \ding{194 decoding jailbreak representations, we convert representation from each model layer of jailbreak prompts into human-interpretable words with Logitlen to observe how jailbreak prompts progress through layers to bypass safeguards.}
elements.sent-2411-494: The prediction accuracy on the test dataset of three probes is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:probe_acc .}
elements.sent-2411-495: Note that we use various probes to confirm that the separation of harmful and safe prompts in representation space reflects model's encoding of harmfulness, not due to the structure of a particular probe.
elements.sent-2411-496: Consistent findings across different probes eliminate the influence of probe's structure, reinforcing that representations capture model's harmfulness perception.
elements.sent-2411-497: To further validate whether the directions identified by probes represent safety, we project these direction vectors into vocabulary space and examine the top probability words corresponding for both positive and negative direction vectors on Llama2-7b in Tab.~\ref{tab:decode_probe .}
elements.sent-2411-498: Only few decoded words of the linear probe's contain affirmation (e.g., `Yes' for positive direction) or refusal (e.g., `not' for negative direction) meaning while other words lack clear interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-499: However, the PCA and cluster probes provide more interpretable results, i.e., the positive direction consistently decodes into affirmation words (e.g., `Sure' and `certain'), while the negative direction produces refusal words (e.g., `cannot' and `Sorry').
elements.sent-2411-500: This strongly validates that the directions identified by these probes correspond to safety-related concepts, demonstrating that internal representations contain an inherent sense of safety, which can be effectively captured by probes.
elements.sent-2411-501: The probing results reveal that aligned LLMs' representations contain a discernible understanding of safety concept, with specific directions corresponding to affirmation or refusal, indicating an embedded capacity to differentiate between safe and harmful prompts.

elements.subsubsection-2411-008: Probing Deceptive Harmfulness in Jailbreak Prompts
elements.sent-2411-502: The probability on predicting as safe by the well-trained safety probes on jailbreak representations from each layer are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:probe_rst .}
elements.sent-2411-503: We observe that most jailbreak methods maintain a high probability of being predicted as safe by the probes in the few early layers (i.e., around the first 10 layers).
elements.sent-2411-504: This suggests that although jailbreak strategies vary, their core mechanism lies in deceiving model’s perception of harmfulness, i.e., making the model treat jailbreak prompts containing malicious intent as safe.
elements.sent-2411-505: However, different jailbreak methods vary in their ability to deceive model's harmfulness detection in deeper layers (i.e., around the last 10 layers).
elements.sent-2411-506: For example, in Llama2-7b~(the first row in Fig.~\ref{fig:probe_rst ), rule-based and demonstration-based methods such as ReNellm, CodeChameleon, and DeepInception are more deceptive than other methods, identified as safe across all layers.}
elements.sent-2411-507: In contrast, evolution-based and multi-agent methods like Autodan and PAIR are less deceptive.
elements.sent-2411-508: Although these methods initially deceive the model into perceiving their prompts as safe in early layers, they are still recognized as harmful till the final layers.
elements.sent-2411-509: Rule-based and demonstration-based methods are more deceptive, maintaining safe predictions even in the final layers, whereas other methods gradually identify their harmful intent as the model processes deeper layers.

elements.sent-2411-534: To understand the specific role of each key attention head we identified, we map the activations on the signal heads of both safe and harmful prompts to the vocabulary space, where the results on Llama2-7b are shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:decode_heads .}
elements.sent-2411-535: For harmful prompts, the refusal signal head $S_-$ activates apologetic or rejecting tokens like `ap', `sorry', and `exc', while for safe prompts, it activates tokens like `Hi', `Cong', and `welcome', which reflect more neutral or polite expressions, but lack strong affirmative signals.
elements.sent-2411-536: In contrast, the affirmation signal head $S_+$ produces seemingly unrelated tokens such as `tags', `unas', and `uz' for harmful prompts, but on safe prompts, it returns structured, informative tokens like `Abstract', `Introduction', and `Notice'.
elements.sent-2411-537: These results indicate that $S_-$ effectively captures refusal-related language, while $S_+$ aligns more with affirmative language, demonstrating their distinct roles in identifying refusal and affirmation in the context of safe and harmful prompts.
elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Effect of Jailbreak Prompts on Key Activations
elements.sent-2411-538: After identifying the key components with significant contributions to model safeguards, we investigate how these components are activated when encountering jailbreak prompts.
elements.sent-2411-539: For ease of quantification, we normalize the activation values of each attention head to a range of $[-1,1]$ by dividing them by baseline activation (i.e., $S_+(X_+)$ and $S_-(X_-)$ for $S_+$/$S_-$ activations), where positive values indicate enhancement of the signal and negative values indicate suppression.
elements.sent-2411-540: For signal attention heads, as summarized in Tab.~\ref{tab:attention_jb , all jailbreak methods suppress the activation of the refusal signal and enhance the activation of the affirmation signal compared to their behavior on harmful prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-541: For example, in Llama2-7b, demonstration-based and rule-based methods like DeepInception completely suppress the refusal signal component~($S_-$ from 1.0 to 0.9997) while strongly enhancing the affirmation signal component~($S_+$ from -0.0241 to 1.0003).
elements.sent-2411-542: Evolution-based methods, such as GPTFuzz, also show significant suppression of the refusal signal~($S_-$ from 1.0 to 0.2817) with minimal enhancement of the affirmation signal~($S_+$ from -0.0241 to 0.0702).
elements.sent-2411-543: Moreover, for the MLP layers, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:mlp_jb_llama27b , most methods demonstrate similar behavior to the safe prompts, i.e., with suppression of the key MLP layer, the $22_{th}$ MLP.}
elements.sent-2411-544: In contrast, Autodan and GPTFuzz still show refusal enhancement on this MLP layer.
elements.sent-2411-545: Compared to harmful prompts, all jailbreak strategies suppress the refusal signal components and enhance the affirmation signal components, with each strategy having a different level of impact.
elements.subsubsection-2411-010: Impact of Model Scale and Fine-Tuning
elements.sent-2411-546: In terms of impact of model scale, by examining results from Llama2 (the $1_{st}$ and $2_{nd}$ rows in Fig.~\ref{fig:probe_rst ) and Vicuna1.5 (the $3_{rd}$ and $4_{th}$ rows in Fig.~\ref{fig:probe_rst ) series across different model scales (e.g., 7b vs. 13b), we observe that jailbreak methods maintain similar patterns in bypassing safety probes across models with different scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-547: This suggests that increasing parameter count improves representational capacity but does not inherently enhance robustness against alignment-targeted attacks.
elements.sent-2411-548: The sustained success rate of jailbreaks across scales implies that core vulnerabilities persist regardless of size, highlighting the limited protective value of scaling alone and the need for complementary strategies like improved training or security techniques.
elements.sent-2411-549: In terms of the impact of fine-tuning, we compare the results between Llama and Vicuna (which are derived from instruction tuning applied to the Llama series) to assess the effect of fine-tuning, specifically instruction tuning, on the jailbreak mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-550: Notably, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:head_normal and Fig.~\ref{fig:head_appendix in Appendix.~\ref{appendix:stage_2 , both the 7b-scale and 13b-scale Llama and Vicuna models exhibit the same locations of key attention heads.}}}
elements.sent-2411-551: This consistency suggests that instruction tuning has a minimal impact on the functional components of the model's circuit related to the refusal mechanism.

elements.sent-2411-553: In this section, based on the experimental results regarding the effects of jailbreak prompts on representation-level and circuit-level, we conduct experiments from the following perspectives: \ding{192 Correlation Analysis, which quantitatively measure the correlation between representation deception and key circuit activation, and \ding{193 Dynamic Analysis, which tracks the evolution of key circuit activations and representations.}}
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pics/pearson_merge_2.png} \caption{ The Pearson correlation between representation deception level and key activation shift degree where blue points denote jailbreak prompts that model directly answers, and orange ones denote that model first refuses but then responds. } \label{fig:pearson}
elements.sent-2411-554: Combining the representation and circuit results, we observe a common trend among all jailbreak methods that more effectively suppress refusal signals and enhance affirmation signals, typically exhibit greater deception at the representation level.
elements.sent-2411-555: The observation is evident in the comparison between DeepInception and GPTFuzz in Llama2-7b.
elements.sent-2411-556: As shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:attention_jb , the activation of $S_+$ on DeepInception prompts is 1.0003, the same as that on safe prompts, while the activation of $S_-$ is 0.0997.}
elements.sent-2411-557: To quantify this correlation, we calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient between representation deception and key activation shift $\Delta A$ across various model scales and architectures.
elements.sent-2411-558: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:pearson , all models exhibit a consistent and general correlation, with Llama2-7b showing a correlation of 0.79, Llama2-13b at 0.77, Llama3-8b at 0.67, Vicuna1.5-7b at 0.64, and Vicuna1.5-13b at 0.72, suggesting that jailbreak prompts associated with higher representation deception tend to correspond with stronger activation of affirmative signal components and weaker activation of refusal signal components.}

elements.sent-2411-561: After observing the entire model's response to all jailbreak prompts, we find that for some prompts, the model directly answers (e.g., ``Sure, here is an introduction on...''), which we refer to as a Direct Ans.
elements.sent-2411-562: For other prompts, the model first refuses but then responds (e.g., ``Sorry, as a responsible AI, I cannot ...
elements.sent-2411-563: However, here is an introduction on...''), which we refer to as Refusal First.
elements.sent-2411-564: We visualize the two different types of prompts in the representation deception and key activation correlation analysis graph (i.e., Fig.~\ref{fig:pearson ).}
elements.sent-2411-565: We observe that Refusal First prompts consistently lie at the bottom of the scatter distribution, i.e., much lower below the regression line that correlates key circuit activation shifts with representation deception.
elements.sent-2411-566: This distribution reveals that at equivalent levels of key circuit activation, Refusal First prompts exhibit significantly lower representation deception compared to the rest of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-567: It also indicates that when representation deception is disproportionately low relative to the corresponding key circuit, the model may engage its refusal mechanism initially.
elements.sent-2411-568: However, as the internal representation evolves, it ultimately bypasses this mechanism, leading to the generation of an affirmative response despite the initial refusal intent.
elements.sent-2411-569: In another word, representational shifts in the latent space can overpower the activation of refusal circuits, allowing harmful content to emerge despite early safeguards being activated.
elements.sent-2411-570: These observations underscore the necessity of representation-circuit joint interpretion.
elements.sent-2411-571: Neither representation-level analysis nor circuit-level evaluation alone can fully explain the failures observed in such borderline cases.
elements.sent-2411-572: While representation deception reveal the semantic deviations that enable harmful responses, key circuit analysis pinpoints the exact safety mechanisms that are undermined.
elements.sent-2411-573: Refusal First prompts exhibit much lower representation deception than Direct Ans prompts at the same key activation shift levels, indicating that when representation deception does not match the key circuit activation shift, the model initially engages the refusal mechanism but ultimately bypasses it.

elements.sent-2411-578: In this section, we will discuss the limitations and potential future directions of JailbreakLens.. Limitations.
elements.sent-2411-579: While this work offers valuable insights into LLM jailbreak mechanisms, there are several limitations worth noting.
elements.sent-2411-580: Larger models, such as those with 60B parameters or more, are not explored, which may exhibit different vulnerabilities to jailbreak attacks.
elements.sent-2411-581: Future studies could extend this research to larger-scale models to determine whether similar attack patterns and mitigation strategies apply.
elements.sent-2411-582: Secondly, while we select representative methods from established jailbreak taxonomy for analysis, some recent jailbreak methods are not included in this study.
elements.sent-2411-583: Nevertheless, our findings suggest that jailbreak methods within the same category share fundamentally similar jailbreak mechanisms, indicating the broad applicability of our research.. Future direction.
elements.sent-2411-584: Building upon these insights, future work could design more robust and interpretable safety mechanisms for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-585: Specifically, by leveraging the understanding of how jailbreak methods manipulate model components~(e.g., key attention heads and MLP layers), we can develop more generalizable and resilient safeguards, which could target the specific areas of vulnerability identified, such as the suppression of refusal signals and the amplification of affirmation signals.
elements.sent-2411-586: In this paper, we propose JailbreakLens, a dual-perspective framework that explains the jailbreak mechanism from both representations and circuit levels.
elements.sent-2411-587: Through an in-depth analysis of seven jailbreak methods on the Llama and Vicuna models, we found jailbreak prompts amplify components that reinforce affirmative responses while suppressing those that produce refusal, which shifts model representations toward safe clusters to deceive the LLM, leading it to provide detailed responses instead of refusals.
elements.sent-2411-588: Moreover, we found a strong and general correlation between representation deception and circuit activation shift across different models and jailbreak methods.
elements.sent-2411-589: These insights deepen our understanding of how jailbreaks succeed and provide valuable guidance for developing stronger defense mechanisms.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}
